title: Payment System Externalities
abstract: We examine how the payment processing role of banks affects their lending activity. In our model, banks operate in separate zones, and issue claims to entrepreneurs who purchase some inputs outside their own zone. Settling bank claims across zones incurs a cost. In equilibrium, a liquidity externality arises when zones are sufficiently different in their outsourcing propensities—a bank may restrict its own lending because it needs to hold liquidity against claims issued by another bank. Our work highlights that the disparate motives for interbank borrowing (investing in productive projects and managing liquidity) can have different effects on efficiency.

article.introduction:
There are large payment flowsbetween banks. For example, in 2017, the Federal funds market had an average daily volume of $75 to $100 billion. Similarly, the Clearing House Interbank Payments System, the largest private clearing house in the United States, processes transfers of $1.5 trillion per day.1In this paper, we ask: what is the role of banks in the payment system, and how does this role affect their lending function?
To address these questions, we construct a stylized model with three important elements. First, banks process payments that generate interbank obligations. As in practice, claims issued on accounts at one bank may be deposited and withdrawn at another bank. Second, payment flows are tied to the level of demand deposits held by banks. Some demand deposits originate from household savings, but banks also create demand deposits when they issue loans. Third, banks are strategic and take these flows into account. To capture the role of the payment system in normal times, our model has no uncertainty (and hence no bank insolvency) and identical productive opportunities across banks.
Our first main result is that a liquidity externality exists across banks in normal times. Because banks are connected through the payment system, claims on one bank, bankA$A$can be cashed in at another bank, bankB$B$. Clearing and settlement are asynchronous in our model, which requires bankB$B$to hold liquid assets against the claims of bankA$A$in the short term. BankB$B$thus loses some control over its own liquidity, and as a consequence restricts the quantity of loans it makes. The bank thus incurs a real cost.
In our framework, if there is no settlement cost, the planning outcome features equal investment across banks. However, in equilibrium, if the flow of claims is not symmetric across banks, investment is distorted away from this outcome. Suppose the claims of bankA$A$are more likely to be redeemed at bankB$B$than vice versa. Then bankA$A$lends more than in the planning outcome, and bankB$B$correspondingly cuts back on loans, that is, a cross-sectional redistribution of economic activity obtains. Interestingly, we find that in many situations, the liquidity externality leads to bankB$B$borrowing from bankA$A$in the interbank market even though, relative to the planning outcome, bankB$B$has decreased lending to the real sector. Our paper therefore highlights that interbank borrowing is not driven solely by differences in productivity, but can also arise from differences in spending patterns that lead to differences in liquidity needs across banks.
Both in our model and in practice, interbank settlement is costly. Many payment systems require banks to post collateral, typically proportional to payment outflows, or to prefund expected outflows.2We assume in our model that net interbank claims incur a settlement cost that is borne by a bank if it is a net payer. In equilibrium, the settlement cost dampens the liquidity externality because each bank is reluctant to be a net payer at the eventual settlement date. Of course, total welfare is reduced by the additional settlement cost.
A planner maximizing total surplus has an incentive to ensure that bank payment flows are symmetric, to reduce the total settlement cost. Therefore, per dollar of investment, if bankA$A$is more likely to generate claims that are cleared by bankB$B$than vice versa, the planner reduces lending by bankA$A$and increases lending by bankB$B$. Thus, the planning solution yields a very different distribution of real investment than the market equilibrium.
Our model features two banks that each operate in their own local zone. Each bank makes loans to a local continuum of entrepreneurs by issuing claims, or “fountain pen money.”3That is, the quantity of lending may exceed the amount of physical currency deposited at the bank. Entrepreneurs use these claims to purchase inputs from households for their production process. Importantly, some inputs must be outsourced, that is, purchased outside the zone in which the lending bank operates. We assume the outsourcing propensity varies across the two zones.
The need for outsourcing has two consequences. First, households holding claims on a bank in another zone may cash them in at their own local bank. This interim demand for liquidity generates a need for interbank loans and in equilibrium constrains the amount of claims banks can issue. The interest rate in the interbank loan market adjusts to clear the market, with banks acting as price-takers in this market. Second, at the end of the game, there is a need to settle up across zones. After netting out claims, one bank may need to transfer cash to the other bank. At this point, consistent with practice, the net payer incurs a settlement cost.
There are two basic frictions in the model. First, interim liquidity needs of consumers, together with strategic behavior by banks, constrain bank lending. Second, banks face a deadweight cost to being a net payer in the payment system. This cost comprises both the collateral cost of using the current system and the internal opportunity cost of liquidity management. The cost associated with being a net payer reduces a bank's willingness to issue claims.
The timing of the model, with liquidity needs arising before final settlement, reflects the asynchronous nature of clearing and settlement. With many interbank transactions, settlement occurs with a lag. Sometimes the lag is short, on the order of intraday. In other cases, such as with banker's acceptances or lines of credit, the settlement lag is longer. This timing of payments matters—as Afonso and Shin (2011) point out, banks generally hold low amounts of cash and reserves and hence they rely on the inflows from other banks to fund the bulk of their own payment outflows.
Modernizing the payment system and reducing the cost to participants has been the subject of much discussion among central bankers and practitioners. One innovation currently under consideration is Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC), discussed in SectionIIIbelow. If wholesale CBDC reduces the settlement cost across banks, our model implies that although it increases welfare overall, it exacerbates the liquidity externality. The result is that inequality in lending across zones is increased rather than decreased. To the extent that a regulator is concerned with regional equity, this is a stark result.
Much of the prior banking literature focuses on frictions related to insolvencies and to asymmetric information. Instead of considering such extreme outcomes, in this paper we abstract from such frictions and examine the day-to-day operations of solvent banks in their dual role as payment processors and lenders to the real economy. In this context, we present a new liquidity externality that is a part of normal bank operations.
Our analysis differs from the seminal work of Diamond and Dybvig (1983) in two fundamental respects. First, as we are interested in frictions other than bank insolvency, our banks face no project risk, in the sense that patient consumers in our model do not have the option to withdraw their deposits at the intermediate date. Second, banks in the Diamond and Dybvig (1983) world are simply a conduit to channel savings from households to the real economy, while we allow banks to issue fountain pen money, which implies that banks must actively choose the quantity of loans they extend. In the latter vein, Donaldson, Piacentino, and Thakor (2018) consider the features of banks that make them the optimal entity to issue claims, and Gu et al. (2013) show that in a model with limited commitment, banks may emerge as trustworthy agents, with claims on bank deposits being used as a means of payment.
Our paper is most closely related to the literature on liquidity management in the banking system. We build on the insights of Freixas, Parigi, and Rochet (2000), who model an interbank payments system based on depositors having to travel to distant places to consume. Their paper focuses on the role of lines of credit in enhancing the resiliency of the banking system when a bank can be insolvent, as such contracts allow exposed banks to spread losses to other banks. We motivate the need for interbank payments by having some producers travel to another location. More importantly, the main insight from our model is that the commitment inherent in an interbank line of credit (which commits a bank to honoring claims issued by another bank) restricts lending ex ante.
Kahn and Roberds (2009) provide an introduction to the economics of payment and settlement systems in the modern economy, and highlight the role of informational frictions. Bianchi and Bigio (2017) present a calibrated general equilibrium model in which banks receive deposits, make loans, and settle reserves in the interbank market. They focus on the importance of liquidity management in the presence of credit demand and other shocks. By contrast, we focus on the effect of strategic credit supply in the absence of uncertainty or informational frictions.
The costs and benefits of gross versus net settlement are examined by, among others, Kahn and Roberds (1998). Bech and Garratt (2003) consider strategic issues in intraday liquidity management in a real-time gross settlement system. Our time horizon is somewhat longer as we consider the period over which banks issue loans to the real economy. Thus, we limit attention to net settlement in our model.
Empirically, Ashcraft, McAndrews, and Skeie (2009) present a detailed analysis of the interbank market, and show that large banks typically borrow from small banks and do so as a result of liquidity shocks that arise due to large value transfers. Craig and Ma (2021) show that, in the German interbank market, some banks are persistent borrowers and others are persistent lenders, consistent with our model.
The paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we introduce our model. We describe the market equilibrium in SectionII. SectionIIIconsiders an application to CBDC, and SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. Model:
Consider an economy with entrepreneurs, households, and banks. The main interactions in the model occur between two banks. Associated with a bank is a continuum of entrepreneurs and a continuum of households, each with unit mass. The bank acts as a monopolist in its own segmented market. Such segmentation could arise from either geographic differentiation or product differentiation along an unspecified dimension. We refer to a bank and its system of entrepreneurs and households as a zone. To justify banks behaving as price-takers in the interbank loan market, we assume that the economy consists of a large numberN$N$of regions, each of which has two banks.
There are three dates,t=0,1,2$t=0,1,2$. There is a single consumption good in the economy, and prices at each time are denominated in units of the consumption good. At time 0, each household in each zone owns a quantity of cashC$C$. Moreover, each household is endowed with an unlimited quantity of labor at time 0 and time 1, and also has access to a linear production technology that produces a unit of the consumption good for every unit of labor that is provided. Households obtain utility from consumptionc$c$and disutility from laborw$w$, withu(c,w)=c−w$u(c,w)=c-w$. At time 1, a proportionλ$\lambda$of households learn they are impatient and value consumption only at that date, and the remainder1−λ$1-\lambda$of households learn they are patient and value consumption only at date 2. Thus,c$c$represents consumption att=1$t=1$for the impatient households and att=2$t=2$for the patient households. The total labor effort isw=w0+w1$w=w_0+w_1$, wherewt$w_t$represents effort att=0,1$t=0,1$.
Cash is directly consumable at date 2, but not at date 0 or 1. Cash does not decay, but households have a small cost to storing cash across time, denoted byε>0$\epsilon > 0$. Therefore, at time 0 households deposit their cash into the bank in their zone.
At time 0, the bank in each zone makes loans in the form of take-it-or-leave-it offers to its local entrepreneurs. The interest rate on the loans is set by the bank and is zone-specific. The loans are to be paid in the consumption good at time 2. To make loans, the bank issues private “inside money,” or bank claims, to entrepreneurs as demand deposits. The entrepreneurs use these claims to purchase inputs from households. Bank claims, denoted byb$b$, correspond to Tobin's (1963) fountain pen money. Typically, the loans extended exceed the cash deposits—entrepreneurs' projects can generate a surplus, creating the resources necessary to repay the higher loan amounts. Letbi$b_i$denote the quantity of bank claims available to an entrepreneur in zonei$i$, andki$k_i$the quantity of inputs she purchases from households. Because there is no danger of bank insolvency, bank claims are not discounted, so each unit of claims is valued at one unit of the consumption good.
Also at time 0, the interbank market is active and thus banks can borrow from or lend cash to each other. This is the market for interim liquidity.4We denote the interest rate at which the interbank loan market clears byr$r$. We assume that the number of regionsN$N$is large, so that banks act as price-takers in this market.
Each entrepreneur owns a technology that produces outputf(k)$f(k)$at time 2 from inputk$k$at time 1, with both output and input consisting of the single good. Inputs must be purchased from households, in exchange for bank claims. To produce the inputs, households use their own labor and their production technology. The linear household technology, combined with an unlimited supply of labor, ensures that all transactions between entrepreneurs and households are at a price equal to one unit of the consumption good. We assume thatf(k)$f(k)$is strictly increasing and strictly concave, and satisfies the Inada conditionslimk→0f′(k)=∞$\lim _{k\rightarrow 0} f^{\prime }(k) = \infty$andlimk→∞f′(k)=0$\lim _{k\rightarrow \infty } f^{\prime }(k) = 0$. The production functionf(k)$f(k)$is the same across all entrepreneurs and all zones. Entrepreneurs are penniless, have zero reservation utility, and provide no effort. Their role in the model is simply to facilitate production—in the model, only the entrepreneurs' technology generates a surplus.
Following Freixas, Parigi, and Rochet (2000), we introduce the need for an interzonal payment system by assuming that entrepreneurs must buy some inputs in the other zone in the same region. The two zones are differentiated by the extent of outsourcing required by the technology. The outsourcing propensity is designated byα∈{αℓ,αh}$\alpha \in \lbrace \alpha _\ell , \alpha _h\rbrace$, where0≤αℓ<αh≤1$ 0 \le \alpha _\ell < \alpha _h \le 1$. We refer to a zone with outsourcing propensityαi$\alpha _i$as zonei$i$, and to the bank in this zone as banki$i$. The entrepreneur in zonei$i$needs a quantity(1−αi)ki$(1-\alpha _i )k_i$of inputs from their own zone and a quantityαiki$\alpha _i k_i$of inputs from zone−i${-i}$. For example, ifαℓ=0$\alpha _\ell = 0$, entrepreneurs in zoneℓ$\ell$only purchase local inputs. Similarly, ifαh=1$\alpha _h = 1$, the entrepreneurs in zoneh$h$purchase all of their inputs from zoneℓ$\ell$.
At time 1, in the morning, entrepreneurs use the bank claims to pay households for the inputs purchased. When entrepreneurs in zoneh$h$(say) purchase inputs from zoneℓ$\ell$, households in the latter zone obtain claims issued by bankh$h$. These households deposit the claims into their own local bank,ℓ$\ell$. Overall, the purchasing process thus results in the claims of a bank with typeαi$\alpha _i$being held by a bank with typeα−i$\alpha _{{-i}}$and vice versa, creating the need for an interzonal payment system.
After having supplied inputs to entrepreneurs, a proportionλ∈[0,1]$\lambda \in [0,1]$of households realize a need for consumption at time 1, before the output from the entrepreneur's technology is realized. These impatient households arrive at their bank in the morning of time 1 and withdraw cash against their bank claims. They also withdraw the cash they had deposited at time 0. Each banki$i$therefore needs to have enough cash on hand (obtained at date 0 from either household deposits or borrowing from the other bank) to satisfy this demand. A proportion1−λ$1-\lambda$of households are patient and do not consume at this interim date. The cash withdrawn by the impatient households is used to purchase the consumption good from patient households. Patient households produce the good using their own labor, and deposit the cash back into their bank accounts at the end of the day at time 1.
Impatient households' need for cash at time 1 imposes a liquidity constraint on banks, and, as demonstrated later, constrains a bank's lending at time 0. We are effectively assuming that households are willing to supply an entrepreneur with inputs in exchange for bank claims, but are only willing to supply each other with the consumption good in exchange for cash. An alternative assumption is that a proportionλ$\lambda$of households only transact in cash, and to purchase inputs from these households an entrepreneur must convert a proportion of their bank claims to cash at the bank that serves the households.
At time 2, production is realized, final interbank settlements are made, the cash deposits are paid off, and all remaining value is remitted to households. All flows at this point, both to and from a bank, are in terms of the consumption good. When the output is produced, entrepreneurs deliver it to their own bank. In addition, the cash held in the bank,C$C$, is now consumable. Now, ifαℓ≠αh$\alpha _\ell \ne \alpha _h$, interbank settlement is required at this date. Suppose, for example, thatαh=1$\alpha _h= 1$andαℓ=0$\alpha _\ell =0$. In this case, all claims issued by both banks have been turned in by households to bankℓ$\ell$. The claims issued by bankh$h$remain liabilities of bankh$h$, so bankh$h$must transfer the consumption good to bankℓ$\ell$to fulfill these claims. Furthermore, if bankh$h$borrowed $1 from bankℓ$\ell$at date 1, it now owes bankℓ$\ell$a total of1+r$1 + r$, which also requires interbank settlement. At date 2, final remittances are made to households. These remittances include settling the cash deposits by households at times 0 and 1, and paying off the bank claims deposited at time 1 by the patient households. Finally, profits generated by the bank are paid off to bank owners, who may be taken to be an exogenous set of agents not subject to liquidity shocks.
Observe that there are two kinds of interbank financial flows in our model. First, interbank loans are taken out at date 1 and repaid at date 2. We assume that the cost of making such transfers is zero. Second, there are flows related to the real economy, which are generated when households in zone−i${-i}$obtain claims issued by banki$i$. We assume that it is costly for a bank to settle such claims. Specifically, a bank that in net terms is sending funds to another bank at date 2 to settle consumer payments incurs a cost ofτ>0$\tau > 0$per unit of funds transmitted. We discuss the interpretation of the settlement costτ$\tau$inAppendix. The timeline is presented in Figure1.
Consider the outcome under autarky. A bank has total funds ofC$C$available to pay out to impatient households at date 2. Suppose the bank issues a quantityb$b$of bank claims. Given that a proportionλ$\lambda$of households are impatient, total withdrawals from the bank at the interim date areλ(C+b)$\lambda (C + b)$. Therefore, to satisfy the interim liquidity requirement at date 1, it must be the case thatλ(C+b)≤C$\lambda (C + b) \le C$, orb≤(1−λ)Cλ$b \le \frac{(1-\lambda ) C}{\lambda }$. Denote byI=(1−λ)Cλ$I = \frac{(1-\lambda ) C}{\lambda }$the investable funds in each zone, that is, the maximal investment a bank can make under autarky. Across a pair of matched banks in a region, total investable funds are then2I$2I$.
Defineβi=αiαℓ+αh2I$\beta _i = \frac{\alpha _i}{\alpha _\ell + \alpha _h} \; 2 I$fori=h,ℓ$i = h,\ell$. Observe thatαℓβh=αhβℓ$\alpha _\ell \beta _h = \alpha _h \beta _\ell$, so if bankh$h$were to issue claims in the quantityβℓ$\beta _\ell$and bankℓ$\ell$in the quantityβh$\beta _h$, there would be no net settlement transfer at date 2. Thus,βh$\beta _h$andβℓ$\beta _\ell$correspond to the investment levels attained if total investable funds across the two zones are allocated in inverse proportion to their respective outsourcing propensitiesαh$\alpha _h$andαℓ$\alpha _\ell$. Becauseαh>αℓ$\alpha _h > \alpha _\ell$, we haveβℓ<I<βh$\beta _\ell < I < \beta _h$.
We impose two parameter restrictions. The first is a restriction on the marginal productivity of the technology in the economy. The second restriction ensures that the cost of interbank transfers through the payment system,τ$\tau$, is not prohibitively large.
Assumption 1.The economy satisfies:


Part (i) of the assumption ensures that in any equilibrium, all investable funds are invested in real projects rather than in the storage technology. Part (ii) is invoked in the proof of Proposition3to ensure that, whenαh$\alpha _h$is sufficiently high compared toαℓ$\alpha _\ell$, there exists an equilibrium in which bankh$h$makes a settlement payment to bankℓ$\ell$.
We think of the period between dates 1 and 2 as a length of time in months or years—this is the time it takes for the entrepreneur to organize production and deliver the final output. In practice, of course, activities such as banks making loans to entrepreneurs, households cashing in bank claims, and projects making payoffs all take place on a daily basis. In the model, date 2 is just a convenient point at which one production cycle ends, and date 1 is a reduced-form way of thinking about all the activity that occurs in the production process over a few months or years, and reflecting the aggregate amount of activity as taking place at one date in the model. When we map the model to the world, intraday activity is an important aspect of how we think about the settlement costτ$\tau$.
In our model, we make a distinction between payment flows related to interbank loans and payment flows related to the settlement of claims between banks. In particular, we set the cost of transfers related to interbank loans to zero, whereas the cost of settling claims isτ≥0$\tau \ge 0$. In practice, both kinds of flows occur daily, and the uncertainty associated with payment streams is larger than that associated with interbank loans. The costτ$\tau$captures the opportunity cost of maintaining extra liquidity or collateral to hedge against the additional uncertainty.
In practice, payment system costs are significantly higher for banks when settling consumer payments than for purely interbank transactions. As Furfine (2011) notes, banks active in the payment system send and receive payments that are 30 times larger than their reserves. Consumer payments are settled throughout the day, so that the associated collateral requirements and especially the net debit caps bite during active trading hours for banks. To facilitate this large volume of payments, the central bank provides intraday credit to each bank. These positions are monitored per minute, and the costs are based on net payments. Thus, as in our model, the costs are incurred by net payers.5
There are both explicit and implicit costs associated with managing intraday liquidity. For example, FedWire in the United States, operated by the Federal Reserve, and CHIPS, a private clearing system, both have explicit (albeit small) fees that must be paid for using the system. More importantly, there are collateral costs as well as limits on payments. For example, FedWire requires collateral on daylight debits (i.e., net outflows), with both a fee of 50 to 150 bps (annualized) on uncollateralized daylight overdrafts and a maximum limit on daylight overdrafts.6Of course, banks may choose to collateralize all overdrafts, but this comes at the opportunity cost of daylight capital. Daylight uses of suitable collateral include obtaining secured financing and meeting margins on derivatives positions. Similarly, CHIPS has a daily prefunding obligation based on expected net outflows. More broadly, under the Liquidity Monitoring tools suggested by the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision, banks are required to manage their intraday liquidity to satisfy both normal payments and stressed demands.7An industry study by consulting firm Oliver Wyman estimates the cost of holding liquidity reserves (both overnight and daylight) to be on the order of 100 bps.8
In contrast, as mentioned by Afonso and Lagos (2014), activity in the federal funds market occurs mostly in the last 2 hours of the operating day (i.e., 16:30 to 18:30 eastern standard time). The loans in this market are unsecured and typically overnight, and collateral required by the payment system is returned to banks at the end of the day. Furthermore, to the extent that some banks are persistent borrowers and others are persistent lenders in this market, the net flows are relatively small. We therefore assume that the cost of transferring these funds is zero, so that the overall cost of transacting in the interbank market is just the interest rate (r$r$in our model).
In the model, a proportionλ$\lambda$of households consume at date 1, and the remaining proportion1−λ$1-\lambda$consume at date 2. While we treat this proportion as exogenous, in practice it can be affected by households' choices over intertemporal consumption. For example, the prospect of inflation between times 1 and 2 may induce households to shift consumption toward time 1, thus increasingλ$\lambda$. Similarly, security innovations that increase trust in the banking system may induce households to leave their claims in until time 2, thus reducingλ$\lambda$. This proportion can also be affected by differential consumption or transaction taxes at the two points in time.
The parameterC$C$represents the level of household savings deposited with banks at time 0. This quantity will depend on household wealth, the level of financial inclusion in the economy, and trust in the banking system. In addition, it will be higher when it is easier for consumers to process transactions with counterparties, for example, through bank-based ACH (automated clearing house) payments and debit or credit cards. On the converse side, strict know-your-customer or KYC rules may drive some people who value privacy to alternate means of payment, leading to a reduction inC$C$.
The bank seeks to maximize its profit. Profit is denominated in units of the consumption good at date 2 and has three components. The first component is the surplus from production, which is captured entirely by the bank. Each bank is a monopolist in its own zone, and thus entrepreneurs are held down to their reservation utility, which is normalized to zero. Suppose banki$i$issues a quantitybi$b_i$of bank claims. Given that claims are converted at a price of one into real inputs, the quantity of inputs purchased,ki$k_i$, equalsbi$b_i$. Therefore, the expected surplus from production isf(ki)−ki=f(bi)−bi$f(k_i) - k_i = f(b_i) - b_i$.
The second component of the profit function is due to interest payments in the interbank market. Letzi$z_i$denote the amount borrowed by banki$i$in the interbank market at date 1. Here,zi<0$z_i < 0$indicates that the bank is a lender rather than a borrower. The cost of borrowing in this market isrzi$r z_i$, where the interest rater$r$is determined in equilibrium, and is taken by banki$i$as given.
In addition, banki$i$may incur a settlement cost to settle claims with bank−i${-i}$at date 2. Recall that entrepreneurs in zonei$i$purchase a quantityαibi$\alpha _i b_i$of inputs in zone−i${-i}$. The quantity of claims of banki$i$that are held by bank−i${-i}$is thereforeαibi$\alpha _i b_i$. Similarly,α−ib−i$\alpha _{-i}b_{-i}$represents the claims of bank−i${-i}$that are held by banki$i$. Thus, the net payment between the banks as a result of such settlement isαibi−α−ib−i$\alpha _i b_i - \alpha _{-i} b_{-i}$. We refer to this amount as a net transfer from banki$i$to bank−i${-i}$. If the net transfer from banki$i$is positive, banki$i$incurs an additional per-unit cost ofτ$\tau$to transmit it through the payment system. This is the third component of the payoff function.
The bank faces an interim solvency constraint at date 1: it must have enough cash or reserves on hand to meet household needs at that date. Households in zonei$i$obtain(1−αi)bi$(1-\alpha _i) b_i$claims from local entrepreneurs andα−ib−i$\alpha _{-i}b_{-i}$claims from distant entrepreneurs. Recall that a proportionλ$\lambda$of households cash in their bank claims at date 1. These households also withdraw the cash they had deposited into the bank at date 0. The demand for interim liquidity at banki$i$therefore amounts toλ(C+(1−αi)bi+α−ib−i)$\lambda (C + (1-\alpha _i) b_i + \alpha _{-i} b_{-i} )$.
To establish a benchmark, we consider the allocation of claims (and hence of investment and production) that would be chosen by a central planner seeking to maximize aggregate output in the economy. As each pair of matched zones is identical, we describe the planner's problem in terms of one such pair. The outcome from a single pair is then replicated across the otherN−1$N-1$pairs.
We show in Proposition1that the liquidity constraints (6) and (7) bind at the optimum. The market-clearing constraint may then be written asbh+bℓ=2I$b_h + b_\ell = 2I$which is easily interpretable as a resource constraint, with2I$2I$being interpreted as the total investable funds across the two banks.
Whenτ>0$\tau > 0$, the planner distorts investment toward the low-outsourcing zone, to try and avoid the settlement costs at date 2. Whenτ>0$\tau > 0$, the planner skews investment toward the low-outsourcing zone. article.II. Market Equilibrium:
We characterize a Nash equilibrium in the banks' lending game, with each bank taking the interest rate in the interbank market,r$r$, as given. Our implicit assumption is thatN$N$is large, so that each zone is small relative to the size of the economy. We restrict attention to equilibria that are symmetric across regions, that is, equilibria in which each high-outsourcing bank issues the same number of claimsbh∗$b_h^*$regardless of region, and similarly for each low-outsourcing bank. We refer to such equilibria as region-symmetric equilibria.

Thus, even though the technologies in the two zones have the same productivity, in a market equilibrium there can be large dispersion in economic activity across the zones. The externality arises because aggressive lending by bankh$h$leads to higher claims deposited at bankℓ$\ell$at the interim date. This tightens the liquidity constraint of bankℓ$\ell$and hence inhibits its own lending. In this way, our model highlights the distinction between clearing (which occurs when bankℓ$\ell$pays out on behalf of bankh$h$) and settlement (which occurs when bankh$h$settles up with bankℓ$\ell$at the end of the game). In foreign trade, banker's acceptances are commonly cleared in a foreign country. As with any externality, an appropriate price or contract can eliminate the negative effect that bankh$h$has on bankℓ$\ell$. In particular, if bankℓ$\ell$can suitably discount claims issued by bankh$h$when it provides either cash (at time 1) or the consumption good (at time 2) to households against these claims, the planning outcome can be recovered. In our model, the only purpose of interbank borrowing is to ensure that a bank has enough liquidity to pay off impatient households at date 3. Consider, for example, a situation in whichαh$\alpha _h$is high, that is,αh>12$\alpha _h > \frac{1}{2}$. All else equal, if bankh$h$expands its lending in this scenario, bankℓ$\ell$needs to hold more liquidity at date 3 than bankh$h$. Thus, bankh$h$lends cash and reserves to bankℓ$\ell$, rather than the other way around.11Therefore, the bank that lends more to the real sector also lends more to the financial sector.
It is important to note that, to isolate the liquidity externality, we assume that the productivity of each zone is the same. If differential productivity were the only source of heterogeneity in the model, then in equilibrium bankh$h$both issues more claims than bankℓ$\ell$and borrows from bankℓ$\ell$in the interbank market. This is the more conventional model of the interbank channel, in which it serves as a device to reallocate resources to more productive banks.
More broadly, therefore, we make the point that borrowing on the interbank market has at least two purposes: first, to fund investments, which leads more productive banks to be borrowers, and second, to fund short-term liquidity needs, which may not be correlated with investment productivity. In the stark case of our model, productivity is the same across banks but the bank that lends less to entrepreneurs has greater need for short-term liquidity—liquidity to honor the claims of the other bank. Our model suggests that to understand borrowing and lending in the interbank market, it is important to distinguish between these different motivations. In particular, a bank's need for funds can be induced through a liquidity externality or can be related to intrinsic demand stemming from access to better investment opportunities.

Notice that we assume that banks are strategic when it comes to lending but are price-takers in the interbank loan market. The literature on relationship lending (see, for example, Petersen and Rajan (1994) or more recently Puri, Rocholl, and Steffen (2017)) finds that informational frictions confer a degree of market power on banks in the loan market. For simplicity, in our model we assume that banks are monopolists in their own zones. In the interbank market, while transactions are bilateral, it is reasonable to think that banks have limited market power. For example, Afonso, Kovner, and Schoar (2014) document that the large concentrated lenders in the U.S. interbank market insure borrowers against supply shocks, rather than raising prices when liquidity is low. In our model, we assume that banks are price-takers in this market. Overall, we assume that it is easier for banks to switch transaction partners in the interbank loan market than for entrepreneurs to switch lenders.
We have explored some other variations of our model. If entrepreneurs have all the bargaining power in their transactions with banks (so banks are effectively price-takers in the loan market as well), the equilibrium is unchanged. In two versions of the model in which banks are also strategic in the interbank market, one based on Marschak and Selten (1974) and the other based on Gabscewicz and Vial (1972), the liquidity externality that we highlight continues to obtain. Finally, if banksh$h$andℓ$\ell$in a given region transact only with each other in the interbank market and can write an enforcible ex ante contract, the planning outcome is recovered in equilibrium.

article.III. Application: CBDC:
The term “central bank digital currency” is used to refer to different forms of digital fiat money. In a Bank for International Settlements (BIS) survey, Barontini and Holden (2019) mention that 70% of the world's central banks are exploring or will soon be exploring CBDC. Bech and Garratt (2017) provide a comprehensive taxonomy that has been adapted and has come to be known as the “BIS money flower” (see Figure3).
We use the term CBDC to refer to token-based digital currencies, or cryptocurrencies, issued by a central bank. Currently, CBDC projects are still at the planning stage, with no large-scale adoption and many possible variations being discussed.14We therefore focus on the broad features of two kinds of CBDC that have been proposed, namely, wholesale and retail CBDC.
Only designated financial institutions will have access to wholesale CBDC. Most projects on wholesale CBDC have been initiated by central banks, but some come from the private sector. Among the former are Project Jasper (Bank of Canada), Project Ubin (Monetary Authority of Singapore), and the Stella project (a joint initiative by the Bank of Japan and the European Central Bank). Among the latter is Utility Settlement Coin, initiated by a private consortium led by UBS with Clearmatics. The distinction relative to the current form of central bank reserves is that transactions in wholesale CBDC will be recorded on a distributed ledger. The expected benefit of wholesale CBDC is that interbank transactions will require less upfront collateral and hence may be more efficient for banks. For example, on implementing Project Inthanon, the Bank of Thailand found that use of wholesale CBDC reduced the opportunity costs associated with intraday liquidity management in the Thai banking system.15
In the context of our model, we interpret wholesale CBDC as an innovation that reduces the settlement costτ$\tau$. Proposition4applies to this case—if the degree to which projects are sourced across regions is widely heterogeneous, regional inequalities in production may become worse when the settlement cost is reduced.
Retail CBDC is envisioned as a new form of central bank money. Consumers will directly hold accounts at the central bank, and can transact with each other using retail CBDC. Examples of retail CBDC include both pilot projects (such as the e-krone in Sweden and the e-peso pilot in Uruguay) and partial adoption such as the e-dinar in Tunisia and the SOV issued by the Marshall Islands. In our model, the availability of retail CBDC reduces the need for cash in the economy. Thus, households deposit less cash in banks, and also withdraw less cash to meet their interim liquidity needs.
As of now, whether retail CBDC will drive out current forms of money completely, or whether it will coexist with cash and bank deposits, is an open question. Agur, Ari, and Dell'Ariccia (2019) consider a model in which some consumers use retail CBDC, whereas others continue to use existing monetary instruments. In our model, the implication would be that some consumers stop using cash. Suppose that retail CBDC is issued, and a proportionϕ$\phi$of consumers switch over to it. Bank deposits fall to(1−ϕ)C$(1-\phi ) C$, and the proportion of consumers needing interim liquidity at datet=1$t=1$falls to(1−ϕ)λ$(1-\phi ) \lambda$. Total investable funds may now be defined asI∼=(1−ϕ)(1−λ)C(1−ϕ)λ=I$\tilde{I} = \frac{(1-\phi ) (1-\lambda ) C}{(1-\phi ) \lambda } = I$. This quantity is invariant toϕ$\phi$, so the partial use of retail CBDC has no effect on lending in our model.
article.IV. Conclusion:
Ensuring a stable and efficient payment system is one of the core principles of banking supervision reforms. For example, the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (2008) has encouraged the adoption of comprehensive rules on liquidity risk in payment systems. In this paper, we show that a bank's liquidity needs can depend in part on the actions of other banks in the payment system. Even though banks can freely trade reserves in an interbank market, strategic considerations affect the allocation of credit, and thus the productive efficiency of the economy. In other words, the amount that a bank lends is critically affected by the fact that it functions as part of the payment system.
A standard intuition is that in the presence of an interbank market to reallocate resources, the marginal product of capital and hence investment will equalize across different production regions. As we demonstrate, if banks are also responsible for ex post settlement in the payment system, this will not occur. Indeed, while we illustrate cross-sectional differences in investment, under a concave production technology aggregate output is also lower than an ideal benchmark level.
In our model, the payment system creates a liquidity externality that distorts lending away from the planning outcome and toward the bank with the higher outsourcing propensity. The settlement costτ$\tau$dampens the extent of this externality, as the cost is incurred by the bank that is a net payer at the end of the game. Reducing the settlement cost (perhaps through wholesale CBDC) is in general welfare-improving, but doing so does have distributional implications and may exacerbate inequalities in lending across zones.
